text:
The European Union’s (EU) use of ‘essential elements’ clauses in its international trade and investment agreements has been a topic of debate. The EU is under an obligation under the Treaty of Lisbon to ensure that its external relations, including its commercial policy, are conducted so as to safeguard its vital interests and, in particular, to promote its values, which include democracy, human rights, and ecomonic and social progress. This trade–human rights nexus has seen the ‘essential elements’ clauses being used as a legal basis for conditionality.

In this article, the author traces the development of the human rights clauses in European Community trade relations from their initial non-binding non-operational nature in the Lomé Conventions to their current elaboration into detailed clauses on “essential elements” of international law and “non-execution” found in the EC's Framework and Free Trade Agreements. The author highlights the EU's endeavours to achieve a certain degree of homogeneity in this regard, but also points out the deficiencies, and obstacles, inherent in the EU's human rights clause practice. These include: - the omission of these provisions in sectoral agreements - certain ambiguities that may arise during the provisional application of treaties - considerable variations in wording, which can severely affect the content of the human rights guarantees provided for in diverse regions and contexts.

One other conclusion we can derive from this report is that there seems to be a discrepancy between EU rhetoric and practice concerning rule of law and human rights protection through the provisions of dispute settlement mechanisms. The clauses have so far been activated only on a case-by-case selective basis, affecting largely ACP countries, while refraining from doing so in relation to significant strategic partners, thus indicating the existence of a “double standard” not mitigated by adequate oversight mechanisms or opportunities for individual complainants. EU trade policy can carry on presenting itself as a forum of moral persuasion regarding human rights provided it continues to acknowledge itself as a conflicted trade power, reconciling as it does the normative aim of defending human rights with the quotidian imperatives of power and profit.
